items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.

items.q: When water boils, what is broken?
items.correct: Intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonds) between water molecules are broken — the O-H covalent bonds stay intact.
items.misconception: The covalent bonds between hydrogen and oxygen atoms are broken when water boils.
items.random_wrong: Water molecules split into hydrogen and oxygen gas when they boil.
items.why: Students don't distinguish between bonds within a molecule and forces between molecules, so they assume boiling 'breaks the molecule apart'.

items.q: When a stress shifts an equilibrium, does the system fully undo the change?
items.correct: No — Le Chatelier's principle says the system PARTIALLY opposes the change; equilibrium shifts to a new position that minimises but doesn't eliminate the disturbance.
items.misconception: The system shifts to completely cancel the change and return concentrations to their original values.
items.random_wrong: The system shifts to amplify the change because reactions always proceed in the disturbed direction.
items.why: Students treat Le Chatelier as a perfect homeostat, missing that the new equilibrium reflects the new conditions and only partially counters the disturbance.
It's surroundings warm up. “surroundings cool” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Combustion of methane. “Thermal decomposition of limestone” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If bonds broken cost 1000 kJ/mol and bonds made release 1300 kJ/mol, what is ΔH (kJ/mol)?
items.why: The real figure is -300 kJ/mol. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
